Industrial Design — Situated Practice — Protocol 04

Lightat theThreshold

Coloured adhesive film cut into a stained-glass pattern and placed on a corridor window — to project coloured light and pull students through a threshold they never knew existed.

01The Place

Most ID students move between studio, elevator, and exit without ever noticing that the corridor continues — past the connecting passage lies the CT building, a whole wing of shared faculty. The corridor is known. The turn at its end is not.

The corridor end — the threshold nobody crosses
02The Protocol

Place a coloured pattern in the window. Let sunlight pass through it. Cast coloured light onto the opposite wall — a signal visible the moment someone steps out of the elevator.

Stained glass was the reference — not its religious weight, but its physics. Coloured film separates white light into wavelengths and projects them forward. The wall becomes a receiver.

03The Installation
Film applied to glass
Colour and geometry
Projected light on floor
View from elevator exit
Full corridor — film in place, waiting for the right angle of sun
04Did It Work?

The intent

Project coloured light onto the wall opposite — visible from the elevator. Make the corridor legible as a path toward something.

The result

The film is visible. Colour diffuses gently. But the strong wall projection — the signal — did not appear during documentation.

Why

Timing. Direct sunlight enters at the correct angle only around 13:00–14:00 on a clear day. The camera wasn't there.

The finding

Not a flaw — a feature. An installation that only performs at noon on clear days makes the light itself the event.

Daily light window

The installation is not always on — it is activated by conditions.

7am
8am
9am
10am
11am
12pm
1–2pm
3pm
4pm
5pm
05Is the Idea Still Right?

Yes. The core concept holds — a passive, light-dependent marker at a threshold to redirect attention. The prototype added specificity: orient the film so the projection lands during peak corridor hours. A refinement, not a departure.

The conditionality is worth keeping. This installation becomes a reason to be in the corridor at a particular time — which is its own kind of protocol.

How does a website add to this?

The installation is time-locked. Without documentation, most people passing through — even at 13:00 — won't know what they're seeing is intentional, or that it behaves differently at another moment.

The website shows the intention behind the object, holds the photographs — the closest substitute for the projected moment nobody captured — and makes the project legible to anyone who will never pass that corridor at noon on a clear day.

The website is not documentation appended to the project. It is a second layer of the protocol — the part that travels, and the part that outlasts the film on the glass.

Light at the Threshold Situated Practice — ID Dept. 2024–2025
